summary: Gruber examines Geoffrey Fowler's investigation into the accuracy of Apple's App Store privacy nutrition labels, which found many apps submitting misleading or false information. While acknowledging the labels depend on developer honesty, Gruber pushes back on Fowler's conclusion that they're useless, drawing an analogy to FDA food nutrition labels which are also self-reported but still valuable. He argues the labels are already motivating developers like PBS to improve their privacy practices. However, he agrees with Fowler that the labels should require developers to disclose which specific third parties receive shared data.
key_points: Fowler's investigation found over a dozen apps with misleading or inaccurate privacy labels, including some sending data to Facebook and Google while claiming high privacy standards
Gruber argues the labels work like FDA food nutrition labels — self-reported but still valuable because penalties and bad publicity motivate compliance
The labels are already driving behavioral change: PBS removed tracking identifiers from its kids app in response to the labeling requirement
Apple can't practically verify every label technically, but that doesn't make the system useless
Gruber agrees the labels have a major gap: they don't require disclosing which specific third parties receive user data
money_quotes: There ought to be penalties for falsifying information on these labels. But it clearly isn't practical for Apple to verify every label for every app in the store.

I don't think that's any different from the mandatory nutrition labels on food products. The FDA doesn't verify those labels — it's the threat of penalties and bad publicity that motivate companies to report accurate information on them.
I don't know anyone who thinks mandatory food nutrition labels are useless, even though surely many of them contain incorrect information.
And if Apple's new privacy labels are useless, why are so many apps making changes to their actual privacy policies?
I think we're already seeing clear evidence that they're motivating developers to remove or reduce privacy-invasive tracking from their apps.
Apple's next crack at these labels should make it mandatory to list exactly which third-parties data is shared with.
key_insight: Apple's privacy labels are imperfect because they rely on developer honesty, but like FDA nutrition labels, their mere existence is already changing developer behavior for the better — and the next iteration should require naming specific third-party data recipients.
